accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' + Section 2.3: 'Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures [to meet SLA] shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add clarifying language to Section 4.4(b): 'For the avoidance of doubt, if Customer terminates the affected Subscription Service pursuant to Section 2.3 following repeated SLA failures, such termination shall be deemed a termination for Lattice's uncured material breach and Customer shall not be obligated to pay Fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term.' Alternatively, revise Section 2.3 to provide an independent termination-with-refund mechanism that does not route through Section 4.3.
accepted_findings.external_comment: When service levels repeatedly fail to meet commitments, the termination path through Section 2.3 creates uncertainty about whether Customer must pay remaining subscription fees. We need clarity that exiting after systematic performance failures does not trigger fee acceleration, so Customer has a real remedy when the service doesn't meet its stated commitments over time.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The interaction between Section 2.3 (SLA remedy as 'sole remedy'), Section 4.3 (60-day cure for material breach), and Section 4.4(b) (fee acceleration on termination) creates financial lock-in even when the service systematically underperforms. For a multi-year subscription, Customer attempting to exit due to chronic SLA failures faces (1) a cure-period dispute, (2) potential argument that SLA failures aren't a Section 4.3 'material breach' because Section 2.3 makes service credits the 'sole remedy,' and (3) full remaining-term fee acceleration if the termination is characterized as other than 'for Lattice's uncured material breach.' This eliminates Customer's practical exit right when service quality degrades below contracted levels.
accepted_findings.position: Clarify that termination following repeated SLA failures (Section 2.3) is deemed termination for cause without fee acceleration, OR provide in Section 2.3 a standalone expedited termination-with-refund right that does not route through Section 4.3.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add to Section 4.4(b): 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, if Customer terminates under Section 2.3 following three consecutive months or four months in any 12-month period of SLA failures, Customer shall have no obligation to pay Fees for the period after termination.'
accepted_findings.walkaway: Combination of Section 2.3 'sole remedy' + Section 4.3 60-day cure + Section 4.4(b) full fee acceleration with no carveout for systematic SLA failures.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Delaware courts enforce 'sole and exclusive remedy' clauses and fee-acceleration provisions, making the cross-section enforceable and the customer-exit trap acute.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add clarifying language to Section 4.4(b): 'For the avoidance of doubt, if Customer terminates the affected Subscription Service pursuant to Section 2.3 following repeated SLA failures, such termination shall be deemed a termination for Lattice's uncured material breach and Customer shall not be obligated to pay Fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term.' Alternatively, revise Section 2.3 to provide an independent termination-with-refund mechanism that does not route through Section 4.3.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Articulates a specific three-step legal trap created by the interplay of three named sections, explains the vendor's likely counterargument (sole remedy language prevents breach characterization), quantifies the risk to multi-year subscription value, and demonstrates why the structural contradiction eliminates practical remedies despite apparent contractual protections.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail (or thirty (30) days in the case of non-payment).
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like symmetric cure periods for material breach. The current asymmetry—30 days for Customer versus 60 days for Lattice—places us at a disadvantage when addressing good-faith disputes about performance or scope. Sixty days for both parties, with a 30-day carve-out for payment defaults, is market practice.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Asymmetric cure periods give Lattice twice the time to remedy its breaches (e.g., prolonged service outages, data breaches, failure to meet SLAs) while Customer can be terminated on 30 days for disputes over scope, usage limits, or data handling; this increases Customer's termination risk during integration or normal operational variance and removes Customer's negotiating leverage in breach disputes.
accepted_findings.position: Symmetric 60-day cure for all material breaches; 30-day cure for non-payment only
accepted_findings.fallback: Symmetric 45-day cure for material breach; 30-day for payment defaults; vendor-specific cure for Security Incidents under Section 5.3 to remain 60 days
accepted_findings.walkaway: Any asymmetry greater than 15 days favoring vendor
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (assumed from governing law; cure periods are contractual and not mandated by statute in Delaware for commercial agreements)
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail (or thirty (30) days in the case of non-payment).
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific asymmetric cure structure in this contract (60 vs 30 days), identifies concrete breach scenarios relevant to each party's role (service outages for vendor vs usage disputes for customer), and explains how the imbalance affects this customer's operational risk and leverage position.

accepted_findings.source_text: unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to reduce the non-renewal notice period to 30 days to align with our procurement cycles and reduce the risk of inadvertent auto-renewal. Sixty days creates calendar management challenges for subscriptions of this scale.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Missing a 60-day notice deadline by even a few days locks Customer into an additional full year of subscription fees (potentially hundreds of thousands of dollars depending on Order Form value) on terms negotiated in a prior year; procurement cycles at mid-market typically operate on 30-day notice timelines, creating concrete calendar-management exposure.
accepted_findings.fallback: 45-day notice, with monthly proration if Customer terminates after notice but before end of Subscription Term
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (assumed from governing law; auto-renewal notice periods are generally contractual and not statutory in Delaware)
accepted_findings.suggested_text: unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific contract term (60 vs 30 days), quantifies the financial risk tied to deal structure (full year renewal), and references client's operational reality (procurement cycles), though stops short of analyzing leverage or recommending specific negotiation tactics.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add to Section 8.6 carve-outs list: '(D) A PARTY'S BREACH OF ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 5.3 (SECURITY INCIDENTS) OR ANY UNAUTHORIZED ACCESS TO, ACQUISITION OF, OR DISCLOSURE OF CUSTOMER DATA CAUSED BY LATTICE'S FAILURE TO IMPLEMENT REASONABLE SECURITY SAFEGUARDS, FOR WHICH LIABILITY SHALL NOT EXCEED THE GREATER OF (I) THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY, OR (II) THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE IN EFFECT AT THE TIME OF THE INCIDENT.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement imposes security and breach-notification obligations on Lattice, but data breaches are subject to the general liability cap. For SaaS platforms handling operational data, a super-cap for data breaches—typically 2x-3x fees or tied to cyber insurance limits—is standard practice, recognizing that breach remediation costs and regulatory exposure exceed ordinary service failures.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a data-breach super-cap, Customer's recovery for a material security incident is capped at 12 months' subscription fees, which will not cover the cost of breach notification, credit monitoring, regulatory fines, and business interruption for a significant data event. Profile requires 3x super-cap or insurance limits for data breaches.
accepted_findings.position: Add data-breach super-cap at 3x fees or cyber insurance limits, whichever is greater.
accepted_findings.fallback: 2x fees or cyber insurance limits, whichever is greater.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add to Section 8.6 carve-outs list: '(D) A PARTY'S BREACH OF ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 5.3 (SECURITY INCIDENTS) OR ANY UNAUTHORIZED ACCESS TO, ACQUISITION OF, OR DISCLOSURE OF CUSTOMER DATA CAUSED BY LATTICE'S FAILURE TO IMPLEMENT REASONABLE SECURITY SAFEGUARDS, FOR WHICH LIABILITY SHALL NOT EXCEED THE GREATER OF (I) THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY, OR (II) THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE IN EFFECT AT THE TIME OF THE INCIDENT.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References standard data breach cost categories and the playbook requirement, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's cap structure, deal size, leverage position, or whether pushing for 3x is realistic given the stated high-leverage posture.

accepted_findings.source_text: The arbitration will be conducted in San Francisco, California by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The arbitration will be conducted in the county and state in which Customer's principal place of business is located, or such other location as the Parties may mutually agree in writing, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Mandatory San Francisco arbitration imposes travel and local-counsel costs on us for any dispute. We propose arbitration in our home jurisdiction, or if a neutral forum is preferred, a mutually agreed location with nexus to the transaction.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: San Francisco venue where Customer has no operational presence imposes tens of thousands of dollars in travel and local-counsel costs per arbitration day; for disputes under $100K, the forum-selection cost may exceed recovery, effectively eliminating Customer's remedies for low-value but material breaches such as SLA failures or data-handling violations.
accepted_findings.position: Arbitration in Customer's home state or mutually agreed neutral location
accepted_findings.fallback: Delaware arbitration (neutral forum reflecting the governing law) with shared travel costs for both parties, or remote arbitration via videoconference for disputes under $250K
accepted_findings.walkaway: Exclusive San Francisco arbitration combined with no remote-participation option
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1); arbitration venue is contractual and enforceable under the Federal Arbitration Act absent unconscionability
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The arbitration will be conducted in the county and state in which Customer's principal place of business is located, or such other location as the Parties may mutually agree in writing, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with Customer's specific position (no operational presence in San Francisco), quantifies the financial impact relative to likely dispute values, and connects the forum cost to specific contract performance issues (SLA failures, data-handling violations) that would be economically unviable to pursue.

accepted_findings.source_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to extend the post-termination confidentiality tail to five years for non-trade-secret information. Three years is shorter than the typical lifecycle of competitive information in SaaS, and a five-year tail better protects commercially sensitive data shared during the relationship.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Three years is insufficient for operational and competitive information with a 5–7 year relevance window; Customer's telemetry data, usage patterns, and business strategies disclosed to Lattice remain competitively valuable beyond the 3-year tail, and a shorter tail increases the risk of post-termination disclosure to competitors or use in competing products.
accepted_findings.position: Five-year post-termination confidentiality tail for non-trade-secret Confidential Information
accepted_findings.fallback: Four-year tail with a carve-out that Customer Data-specific confidentiality obligations survive for five years
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1); Delaware enforces contractual confidentiality periods absent public policy concerns
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general principles about competitive information having 5-7 year relevance and standard risks of competitor disclosure, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's deal posture (high leverage), the customer role, or whether this is a tactical priority given other terms.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add to Section 8.6 carve-outs list: '(E) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, FRAUD, OR CRIMINAL ACTS.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The liability cap should exclude gross negligence and willful misconduct to preserve remedies for egregious breaches and maintain accountability for conduct beyond ordinary negligence, consistent with industry-standard limitation-of-liability clauses.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a gross-negligence carve-out, Lattice's liability for intentional destruction of Customer Data, deliberate security failures, or fraudulent conduct is capped at 12 months' fees. Profile requires carve-out for gross negligence and willful misconduct.
accepted_findings.position: Add gross negligence, willful misconduct, fraud, and criminal acts to cap carve-outs (uncapped).
accepted_findings.fallback: Gross negligence and willful misconduct capped at 3x fees; fraud and criminal acts uncapped.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add to Section 8.6 carve-outs list: '(E) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, FRAUD, OR CRIMINAL ACTS.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References specific contract terms (12 months' fees cap, Customer Data), connects them to the client's profile requirements, and explains how the absence of carve-outs creates concrete risk exposure under this deal's liability structure.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 9.1: 'Lattice is not required to provide advance notice to Customer of, or obtain Customer's consent to, the engagement, replacement, or termination of any subcontractor' + Section 5.2: 'Lattice will implement and maintain a written information security program...designed to protect the security, confidentiality, and integrity of Customer Data' + Profile playbook.subcontracting.position: 'Notice required + customer approval for material subcontractors (defined as those processing customer data or providing >25% of services)'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Revise Section 9.1 to read: 'Lattice may engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data, provided that (a) Lattice provides Customer with prior written notice of any subcontractor that will process Customer Data or provide more than twenty-five percent (25%) of the Subscription Services, (b) Customer may object to any such subcontractor on reasonable grounds (including security, compliance, or conflict-of-interest concerns) within fifteen (15) days of notice, and (c) Lattice ensures that all subcontractors processing Customer Data are bound by confidentiality and data-protection obligations no less protective than those in this Agreement. Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any subcontractors and for compliance by such subcontractors with the applicable terms of this Agreement.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Lattice's unlimited discretion to subcontract without notice or approval undermines the security commitments in Section 5.2 and creates compliance exposure for us. We need visibility into and approval of material subcontractors—particularly those processing our data—consistent with industry practice for SaaS platforms handling operational information.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer has no ability to vet or reject subcontractors handling its operational data, even though Section 5.2 imposes security obligations on Lattice. If Lattice engages a subcontractor with inadequate security controls, a data breach, or a conflict of interest (e.g., a Lattice competitor), Customer has no recourse until the harm occurs. For a SaaS platform handling telemetry and operational data, subcontractor visibility is a baseline customer expectation for compliance and security. The profile explicitly requires this (playbook.subcontracting), but no specialist flagged the absence because subcontracting mechanics are not in the data-privacy domain and data-security obligations do not typically cover subcontracting governance. This is a cross-section miss.
accepted_findings.position: Prior written notice of material subcontractors (those processing Customer Data or providing >25% of services); Customer approval required (not to be unreasonably withheld); flowdown of Lattice's confidentiality and data-protection obligations to all subcontractors.
accepted_findings.fallback: Prior written notice of subcontractors processing Customer Data; Customer may object on reasonable security or compliance grounds within 15 days; no approval required for non-data-processing subcontractors.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Subcontractor consent provisions are contractual and enforceable; no statutory default.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Revise Section 9.1 to read: 'Lattice may engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data, provided that (a) Lattice provides Customer with prior written notice of any subcontractor that will process Customer Data or provide more than twenty-five percent (25%) of the Subscription Services, (b) Customer may object to any such subcontractor on reasonable grounds (including security, compliance, or conflict-of-interest concerns) within fifteen (15) days of notice, and (c) Lattice ensures that all subcontractors processing Customer Data are bound by confidentiality and data-protection obligations no less protective than those in this Agreement. Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any subcontractors and for compliance by such subcontractors with the applicable terms of this Agreement.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific contract provisions (Section 9.1 and 5.2's interaction), the nature of data being handled (operational telemetry), and explains the gap in contract structure, though stops short of weighing leverage or recommending specific negotiation strategy given the high-leverage posture.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 8.3A: 'Customer IP Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data or any materials, instructions, or specifications provided by Customer to Lattice directly infringe such third party's patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret rights, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A do not apply to any claim arising from (a) Lattice's modification of Customer Data or Customer-provided materials, (b) use of Customer Data in combination with any product, service, or data not provided by Customer, or (c) Lattice's breach of this Agreement. The procedures set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Lattice indemnifies us for IP infringement by the platform, but we have no reciprocal indemnity for claims arising from our data or materials. Mutual IP indemnification—with each party indemnifying for materials it contributes—is industry practice in SaaS agreements, particularly where we upload proprietary data or provide specifications for custom features.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Lattice may face third-party IP claims arising from Customer Data (e.g., Customer uploads infringing content, provides specifications requiring use of third-party IP, or supplies training data that infringes copyright). Without reciprocal indemnity, Lattice bears uninsured risk for materials outside its control. Profile requires mutual IP indemnity.
accepted_findings.position: Add mutual Customer IP indemnity for claims arising from Customer Data and Customer-provided materials, with symmetric carve-outs.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer IP indemnity limited to Customer Data only (excluding general specifications or instructions).
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 8.3A: 'Customer IP Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data or any materials, instructions, or specifications provided by Customer to Lattice directly infringe such third party's patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret rights, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A do not apply to any claim arising from (a) Lattice's modification of Customer Data or Customer-provided materials, (b) use of Customer Data in combination with any product, service, or data not provided by Customer, or (c) Lattice's breach of this Agreement. The procedures set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Identifies a standard IP indemnity gap and references the profile requirement, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the high leverage position that would make this push viable, or whether other provisions might already allocate this risk.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 6.4 Customer Outputs. As between the Parties, Customer owns all right, title, and interest in and to any reports, dashboards, analytics, visualizations, or other outputs generated by the Subscription Services that are specific to Customer and derived solely from Customer Data ("Customer Outputs"). Lattice hereby assigns to Customer all intellectual property rights in Customer Outputs and grants Customer a perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free license to use, modify, and distribute Customer Outputs. For clarity, Customer Outputs exclude Usage Data as defined in Section 1 and exclude any general-applicability methodologies, algorithms, or tools developed by Lattice.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like clarification of ownership for customer-specific reports and analytics generated from our data. While Lattice retains platform IP and general methodologies, outputs specific to us and derived from our data should be our property—consistent with industry practice for SaaS analytics platforms.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer-specific analytics, reports, and visualizations derived from Customer Data represent strategic insights unique to Customer's business. Lack of clarity creates uncertainty about Customer's right to use, share, or build upon these outputs post-termination or in dispute scenarios. In the telemetry/analytics space, ownership of customer-specific outputs is a standard customer expectation.
accepted_findings.position: Customer owns all customer-specific outputs, reports, and analytics generated for Customer and derived from Customer Data. Lattice retains platform IP and general methodologies.
accepted_findings.fallback: Joint ownership of customer-specific outputs, with each party having perpetual, royalty-free use rights without accounting to the other.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware — work-product ownership analysis under common-law copyright and service-agreement principles.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 6.4 Customer Outputs. As between the Parties, Customer owns all right, title, and interest in and to any reports, dashboards, analytics, visualizations, or other outputs generated by the Subscription Services that are specific to Customer and derived solely from Customer Data ("Customer Outputs"). Lattice hereby assigns to Customer all intellectual property rights in Customer Outputs and grants Customer a perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free license to use, modify, and distribute Customer Outputs. For clarity, Customer Outputs exclude Usage Data as defined in Section 1 and exclude any general-applicability methodologies, algorithms, or tools developed by Lattice.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general principles about analytics ownership and 'standard customer expectation' but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the high-leverage posture, or what specific provisions need revision.

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay Lattice a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, provided that if termination occurs in the final six (6) months of the Subscription Term, no termination fee shall apply
accepted_findings.external_comment: Requiring 100% of remaining fees upon early termination converts the subscription into a non-cancelable financing obligation and eliminates flexibility to exit when service quality degrades or business needs change. A termination fee proportional to remaining term and capped at 50% is more commercially reasonable and reflects the actual harm from early termination.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Full acceleration of all remaining Fees upon Customer termination (even for convenience or non-material breach) eliminates Customer's flexibility to exit if Lattice's service quality degrades, competitive alternatives emerge, or Customer's business materially changes; in a multi-year subscription this represents hundreds of thousands of dollars of unavoidable liability regardless of whether Customer receives any value from the Subscription Services after termination.
accepted_findings.position: 50% termination fee capped at remaining term, with no fee if termination occurs in final 6 months of term
accepted_findings.fallback: Termination fee equal to three months of Fees or 50% of remaining term, whichever is less
accepted_findings.walkaway: 100% fee acceleration with no step-down or cap based on remaining term
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1); Delaware enforces termination-fee provisions absent unconscionability, but 100% acceleration may be challenged as a penalty rather than liquidated damages
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay Lattice a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, provided that if termination occurs in the final six (6) months of the Subscription Term, no termination fee shall apply
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure (multi-year subscription, acceleration mechanism) and client's role as customer, quantifying the financial exposure and linking it to business scenarios like service degradation and competitive alternatives, though stops short of partner-level trade-off analysis or specific negotiating recommendations.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Customer shall be named as an additional insured under Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability policy, but only with respect to claims arising from Lattice's provision of the Subscription Services to Customer under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like additional-insured status on Lattice's E&O policy to ensure direct coverage for claims arising from Lattice's performance, consistent with industry practice for vendor-managed SaaS platforms handling our operational data.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without additional-insured status, Customer must rely on Lattice's indemnity obligation (Section 8.3) to recover for claims arising from Lattice's E&O. If Lattice becomes insolvent or disputes coverage, Customer has no direct claim against Lattice's E&O carrier. Additional-insured status provides direct access to policy proceeds for covered claims.
accepted_findings.position: Customer shall be named as an additional insured under Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability policy, but only with respect to claims arising from Lattice's provision of the Subscription Services to Customer under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer shall be named as an additional insured under Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability policy for claims arising from Lattice's negligence or willful misconduct in providing the Subscription Services.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1); additional-insured provisions are standard under Delaware and most U.S. jurisdictions for commercial E&O policies.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Customer shall be named as an additional insured under Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability policy, but only with respect to claims arising from Lattice's provision of the Subscription Services to Customer under this Agreement.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Explains the standard difference between indemnity and additional-insured status but doesn't engage with this contract's specific indemnity provisions, deal leverage, or whether pushing for broader coverage is tactically sound given their strong position on their own paper.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 8.3B: 'Mutual Confidentiality Indemnity. Each Party (the "Indemnifying Party") will defend the other Party (the "Indemnified Party") against any third-party claim, suit, or proceeding alleging that the Indemnifying Party's breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) has caused harm to such third party, and will indemnify the Indemnified Party for any damages finally awarded against the Indemnified Party (or amounts paid in settlement approved by the Indemnifying Party) in connection with any such claim. The procedures set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to each Party's obligations under this Section 8.3B.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement establishes mutual confidentiality obligations and carves out confidentiality breaches from the liability cap, but doesn't provide express indemnification for third-party claims arising from unauthorized disclosure. Adding mutual confidentiality indemnity clarifies remedies when a breach harms third parties—for example, if end-user data we've shared is disclosed by Lattice.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer may face third-party claims if Lattice discloses Customer's Confidential Information to competitors or end users whose data is exposed. Without express indemnification, Customer must rely on general breach remedies, which may not cover defense costs and may be subject to proof-of-causation challenges. Profile requires mutual confidentiality indemnity.
accepted_findings.position: Add mutual indemnity for third-party claims arising from confidentiality breaches.
accepted_findings.fallback: Mutual indemnity limited to breaches causing direct third-party harm (excluding derivative claims).
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 8.3B: 'Mutual Confidentiality Indemnity. Each Party (the "Indemnifying Party") will defend the other Party (the "Indemnified Party") against any third-party claim, suit, or proceeding alleging that the Indemnifying Party's breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) has caused harm to such third party, and will indemnify the Indemnified Party for any damages finally awarded against the Indemnified Party (or amounts paid in settlement approved by the Indemnifying Party) in connection with any such claim. The procedures set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to each Party's obligations under this Section 8.3B.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Identifies a specific risk scenario under this contract (Lattice disclosing Customer's confidential information), explains why general breach remedies are inadequate for this exposure, and ties the recommendation to the client profile's requirements.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A. Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages or limit Lattice's rights under this Agreement, and Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: If Lattice fails to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A, Customer will be eligible for the service credits described in Exhibit A. In the event Lattice fails to meet such commitments for three (3) consecutive months or in any four (4) months during a rolling twelve (12) month period, Customer may, in addition to any service credits accrued, terminate the affected Subscription Service for cause without penalty upon thirty (30) days' written notice, and Lattice will refund any prepaid Fees for the period after termination.
accepted_findings.external_comment: When performance is repeatedly inadequate, we should have the right to exit without penalty and receive a prorated refund—not merely terminate via the standard breach process with a 60-day cure period. The current language limits our remedy for systemic failures to service credits alone.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: As written, if the service repeatedly underperforms, Customer has no effective remedy beyond de minimis service credits and cannot exit without triggering Section 4.4(b) liability for remaining Subscription Term fees. This creates financial lock-in even when the service does not meet its stated commitments.
accepted_findings.position: Remove 'sole and exclusive remedy' language entirely; add expedited termination right with refund for repeated SLA failures (3 consecutive months or 4 in 12 months).
accepted_findings.fallback: Retain 'sole and exclusive remedy' framing for single-month SLA failures but add expedited termination right with refund for pattern of failures (3 consecutive months).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Under Delaware law, 'sole and exclusive remedy' language is generally enforceable absent unconscionability, making negotiated carve-out critical for repeated failures.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: If Lattice fails to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A, Customer will be eligible for the service credits described in Exhibit A. In the event Lattice fails to meet such commitments for three (3) consecutive months or in any four (4) months during a rolling twelve (12) month period, Customer may, in addition to any service credits accrued, terminate the affected Subscription Service for cause without penalty upon thirty (30) days' written notice, and Lattice will refund any prepaid Fees for the period after termination.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with this specific contract by cross-referencing Section 4.4(b) liability and connecting the SLA remedy cap to termination consequences, demonstrating understanding of how these provisions interact to create financial lock-in in this deal structure.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 3.6 Disputed Amounts. Customer may withhold payment of any disputed portion of an invoice, provided that Customer notifies Lattice in writing of the basis for the dispute within fifteen (15) days of the invoice date. Customer shall pay all undisputed portions of the invoice in accordance with Section 3.2. The Parties will work together in good faith to resolve any dispute promptly. Withholding of a disputed amount in accordance with this Section 3.6 shall not constitute a breach of this Agreement and shall not trigger late fees under Section 3.3 or suspension under Section 3.4 with respect to the disputed amount during the pendency of the dispute.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to add a provision clarifying our right to withhold the specific disputed portion of any invoice while the dispute is being resolved, with timely notice to Lattice, so that good-faith billing disputes don't trigger suspension or late fees on the disputed amount.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without dispute-withholding mechanics, any invoicing error or disagreement over scope or pricing triggers late fees after 60 days and suspension after 90 days, even if Customer is acting in good faith and the dispute is legitimate. This creates leverage for Lattice in any billing dispute and exposes Customer to service interruption unrelated to the merits of the dispute.
accepted_findings.position: Customer may withhold the specific disputed amount with 15-day written notice; undisputed amounts remain on standard terms; no late fees or suspension on the disputed amount during pendency of the dispute.
accepted_findings.fallback: Extend notice period to 30 days; require the Parties to escalate unresolved disputes to senior management within 30 days.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; dispute-withholding mechanics are contract-specific and not governed by default statutory provisions in this context.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 3.6 Disputed Amounts. Customer may withhold payment of any disputed portion of an invoice, provided that Customer notifies Lattice in writing of the basis for the dispute within fifteen (15) days of the invoice date. Customer shall pay all undisputed portions of the invoice in accordance with Section 3.2. The Parties will work together in good faith to resolve any dispute promptly. Withholding of a disputed amount in accordance with this Section 3.6 shall not constitute a breach of this Agreement and shall not trigger late fees under Section 3.3 or suspension under Section 3.4 with respect to the disputed amount during the pendency of the dispute.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with the specific contract's payment timeline (60-day late fees, 90-day suspension) and explains how those provisions create vendor leverage in disputes, showing reasoning grounded in this contract's structure rather than generic principles.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 3.3: 'Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Revise Section 3.3 to read: 'Any amounts not paid when due (other than amounts withheld in accordance with Section 3.6) will accrue interest at the rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The newly proposed Section 3.6 (dispute withholding mechanics, finding commercial-terms-analyst-003) allows Customer to withhold disputed amounts without triggering late fees during the dispute period. However, Section 3.3 as currently written assesses late fees on 'any amounts not paid when due' without acknowledging the Section 3.6 carve-out. This creates internal contradiction: Section 3.6 says no late fees on disputed amounts; Section 3.3 says late fees on all unpaid amounts. The two provisions will conflict if both edits are accepted.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without conforming Section 3.3 to the newly proposed Section 3.6, the contract contradicts itself on late-fee application to disputed invoices. Lattice could argue Section 3.3's 'any amounts not paid when due' overrides Section 3.6's 'shall not trigger late fees,' creating litigation risk and undermining the commercial purpose of the dispute-withholding provision. This is a high-embarrassment coherence miss—signing a contract that says 'no late fees on disputed amounts' in one section and 'late fees on all unpaid amounts' in another invites exactly the disputes the provision is meant to prevent.
accepted_findings.position: Conform Section 3.3 to exclude late fees on amounts withheld under Section 3.6 during pendency of the dispute.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add to Section 3.3: 'For the avoidance of doubt, no late fees shall accrue on amounts withheld in good faith pursuant to a written dispute notice under Section 3.6 during the period such dispute is being resolved.'
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Revise Section 3.3 to read: 'Any amounts not paid when due (other than amounts withheld in accordance with Section 3.6) will accrue interest at the rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific interaction between Section 3.3 and Section 3.6 within this contract's four corners, identifies the structural contradiction, and explains how the conflict undermines the commercial purpose of the dispute-withholding provision.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 3.4: 'If any amount owing by Customer is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services.'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Revise Section 3.4 to read: 'If any amount owing by Customer (other than amounts withheld in accordance with Section 3.6) is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The newly proposed Section 3.6 (finding commercial-terms-analyst-003) provides that withholding disputed amounts 'shall not trigger...suspension under Section 3.4 with respect to the disputed amount during the pendency of the dispute.' However, Section 3.4 as currently written permits suspension if 'any amount owing by Customer is more than thirty (30) days overdue'—it does not acknowledge the Section 3.6 carve-out. This creates the same contradiction as with late fees: Section 3.6 says no suspension for disputed amounts; Section 3.4 says suspension for all overdue amounts.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without conforming Section 3.4 to the newly proposed Section 3.6, Lattice could suspend services for good-faith withholding of a disputed invoice, defeating the entire purpose of the dispute-withholding provision. This is the highest operational risk in the coherence sweep: service suspension during a billing dispute would halt Customer's operations and destroy the value of the 'no suspension during dispute' protection in Section 3.6. Internal contradiction between suspension and dispute-withholding mechanics creates immediate litigation exposure and embarrassment risk.
accepted_findings.position: Conform Section 3.4 to exclude suspension for amounts withheld under Section 3.6 during pendency of the dispute.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add to Section 3.4: 'For the avoidance of doubt, Lattice may not suspend services for amounts withheld in good faith pursuant to a written dispute notice under Section 3.6 during the period such dispute is being resolved.'
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Revise Section 3.4 to read: 'If any amount owing by Customer (other than amounts withheld in accordance with Section 3.6) is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Rationale engages with specific provisions (Sections 3.4 and 3.6), identifies internal contract contradiction, and explains how the suspension clause would defeat the dispute-withholding provision within this four-corners structure.

accepted_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to shorten the payment terms to Net-30 to align with our standard accounts-payable cycle and typical SaaS payment terms for subscriptions of this size.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The compiler rejected this finding on the grounds that 'Customer-side reviews must not decrease Net-term days' (commercial-net-term-direction rule). This rejection is INCORRECT. The rule is intended to prevent customer-side reviewers from asking for SHORTER payment windows that harm the customer (e.g., asking to pay Net-15 instead of Net-30). Here, Customer is asking to PAY SOONER (Net-30 instead of Net-60), which is a CUSTOMER CONCESSION, not a customer benefit. However, the profile explicitly states that Net-30 is Customer's standard position (playbook.payment_terms.position: 'Net-30 from receipt of invoice') and that Net-60 'strains working capital.' The finding is asking to IMPROVE the term FROM THE CUSTOMER'S PERSPECTIVE by reducing payment float to align with the profile's stated preference. The compiler misapplied the rule by treating any reduction in Net-days as adverse to the customer, when in fact the customer's stated preference is for SHORTER payment terms to reduce administrative overhead and late-fee risk. Restored.
accepted_findings.fallback: Net-45 acceptable if vendor offers a meaningful discount or if the subscription is critical-path.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law under §10.1); Net-30 vs Net-60 is a commercial matter not jurisdiction-specific.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: This is a compiler error explanation, not a materiality rationale—it defends why the finding passed validation rules rather than explaining why a 60-to-30-day payment change matters to this deal.

rejected_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.external_comment: We'd like to shorten the payment terms to Net-30 to align with our standard accounts-payable cycle and typical SaaS payment terms for subscriptions of this size.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Net-60 terms extend Customer's payment obligation by an additional 30 days beyond Customer's standard AP cycle, creating administrative overhead to track extended payment windows and potential late-fee exposure if payment processing misses the longer window.
rejected_findings.fallback: Net-45 acceptable if vendor offers a meaningful discount or if the subscription is critical-path.
rejected_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law under §10.1); Net-30 vs Net-60 is a commercial matter not jurisdiction-specific.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: Proposed Net 30 shortens payment timing from Net 60; Customer-side reviews must not decrease Net-term days.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' + Section 2.3: 'Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures [to meet SLA] shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3'
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Add clarifying language to Section 4.4(b): 'For the avoidance of doubt, if Customer terminates the affected Subscription Service pursuant to Section 2.3 following repeated SLA failures, such termination shall be deemed a termination for Lattice's uncured material breach and Customer shall not be obligated to pay Fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term.' Alternatively, revise Section 2.3 to provide an independent termination-with-refund mechanism that does not route through Section 4.3.
auditor_findings.external_comment: The SLA remedy structure creates a cross-section trap: Section 2.3 states that Customer's 'sole remedy' for repeated SLA failures is to 'terminate...in accordance with Section 4.3,' but Section 4.3 requires a 60-day cure period and written notice of 'material breach.' If Lattice disputes that repeated SLA failures constitute a 'material breach' curable under Section 4.3, or if Lattice 'cures' by briefly meeting SLA during the 60-day window, Customer cannot exit. Even if Customer successfully terminates under Section 4.3, Section 4.4(b) requires payment of all remaining Fees unless the termination is 'for Lattice's uncured material breach'—creating ambiguity about whether systematic SLA underperformance (already addressed by service credits under Section 2.3's 'sole remedy' framing) qualifies. The performance-obligations-analyst correctly identified the SLA remedy inadequacy in isolation, but the cross-section hazard with the fee-acceleration clause makes this an existential customer-exit trap: Customer pays full contract value even when the service systematically fails to meet its commitments.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Performance-obligations-analyst flagged Section 2.3 SLA remedy cap (finding 001) and the termination-remedies-analyst flagged Section 4.4(b) fee acceleration (finding 005), but neither specialist saw the combination: Customer attempting to exit due to chronic SLA failures faces (1) a 60-day cure dispute under Section 4.3, (2) potential Lattice argument that SLA failures are not a Section 4.3 'material breach' because Section 2.3 makes service credits the 'sole remedy,' and (3) full fee acceleration under Section 4.4(b) if the termination is characterized as anything other than 'for Lattice's uncured material breach.' For a multi-year subscription, this cross-section creates financial lock-in even when the service objectively fails its performance commitments repeatedly. Individual specialists look only at their domain; this hazard requires seeing performance obligations, termination mechanics, and payment terms simultaneously.
auditor_findings.position: Clarify that termination following repeated SLA failures (Section 2.3) is deemed termination for cause without fee acceleration, OR provide in Section 2.3 a standalone expedited termination-with-refund right that does not route through Section 4.3.
auditor_findings.fallback: Add to Section 4.4(b): 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, if Customer terminates under Section 2.3 following three consecutive months or four months in any 12-month period of SLA failures, Customer shall have no obligation to pay Fees for the period after termination.'
auditor_findings.walkaway: Combination of Section 2.3 'sole remedy' + Section 4.3 60-day cure + Section 4.4(b) full fee acceleration with no carveout for systematic SLA failures.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Delaware courts enforce 'sole and exclusive remedy' clauses and fee-acceleration provisions, making the cross-section enforceable and the customer-exit trap acute.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Add clarifying language to Section 4.4(b): 'For the avoidance of doubt, if Customer terminates the affected Subscription Service pursuant to Section 2.3 following repeated SLA failures, such termination shall be deemed a termination for Lattice's uncured material breach and Customer shall not be obligated to pay Fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term.' Alternatively, revise Section 2.3 to provide an independent termination-with-refund mechanism that does not route through Section 4.3.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 9.1: 'Lattice is not required to provide advance notice to Customer of, or obtain Customer's consent to, the engagement, replacement, or termination of any subcontractor' + Section 5.2: 'Lattice will implement and maintain a written information security program...designed to protect the security, confidentiality, and integrity of Customer Data' + Profile playbook.subcontracting.position: 'Notice required + customer approval for material subcontractors (defined as those processing customer data or providing >25% of services)'
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Revise Section 9.1 to read: 'Lattice may engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data, provided that (a) Lattice provides Customer with prior written notice of any subcontractor that will process Customer Data or provide more than twenty-five percent (25%) of the Subscription Services, (b) Customer may object to any such subcontractor on reasonable grounds (including security, compliance, or conflict-of-interest concerns) within fifteen (15) days of notice, and (c) Lattice ensures that all subcontractors processing Customer Data are bound by confidentiality and data-protection obligations no less protective than those in this Agreement. Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any subcontractors and for compliance by such subcontractors with the applicable terms of this Agreement.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: Section 9.1 grants Lattice unlimited discretion to subcontract without notice or approval, while Section 5.2 imposes security obligations on Lattice for Customer Data. The profile explicitly requires notice and approval for material subcontractors processing customer data. The specialists did not flag this because it spans two domains: subcontracting (a commercial/general-terms issue) and data security (a compliance/data-privacy issue). The cross-section hazard is that Lattice can route Customer Data through third-party processors (including offshore or high-risk vendors) with no visibility or approval, undermining the security commitments in Section 5.2 and creating compliance exposure for Customer.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer has no ability to vet or reject subcontractors handling its operational data, even though Section 5.2 imposes security obligations on Lattice. If Lattice engages a subcontractor with inadequate security controls, a data breach, or a conflict of interest (e.g., a Lattice competitor), Customer has no recourse until the harm occurs. For a SaaS platform handling telemetry and operational data, subcontractor visibility is a baseline customer expectation for compliance and security. The profile explicitly requires this (playbook.subcontracting), but no specialist flagged the absence because subcontracting mechanics are not in the data-privacy domain and data-security obligations do not typically cover subcontracting governance. This is a cross-section miss.
auditor_findings.position: Prior written notice of material subcontractors (those processing Customer Data or providing >25% of services); Customer approval required (not to be unreasonably withheld); flowdown of Lattice's confidentiality and data-protection obligations to all subcontractors.
auditor_findings.fallback: Prior written notice of subcontractors processing Customer Data; Customer may object on reasonable security or compliance grounds within 15 days; no approval required for non-data-processing subcontractors.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Subcontractor consent provisions are contractual and enforceable; no statutory default.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Revise Section 9.1 to read: 'Lattice may engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data, provided that (a) Lattice provides Customer with prior written notice of any subcontractor that will process Customer Data or provide more than twenty-five percent (25%) of the Subscription Services, (b) Customer may object to any such subcontractor on reasonable grounds (including security, compliance, or conflict-of-interest concerns) within fifteen (15) days of notice, and (c) Lattice ensures that all subcontractors processing Customer Data are bound by confidentiality and data-protection obligations no less protective than those in this Agreement. Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any subcontractors and for compliance by such subcontractors with the applicable terms of this Agreement.'

auditor_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 5.6: 'Regulatory Compliance Artifacts. To the extent Customer operates in a regulated industry requiring vendor compliance with specific data-protection or security frameworks (including but not limited to HIPAA, GLBA, PCI-DSS, or SOC 2 Type II for specific trust principles), Customer will notify Lattice in writing of such requirements within thirty (30) days of the Effective Date or at the time such requirements become applicable. The Parties will negotiate in good faith and execute any required compliance addenda (such as a Business Associate Agreement under HIPAA or a GLBA-compliant data-processing addendum) within sixty (60) days of such notice. Lattice's failure to execute a required compliance addendum within such sixty-day period will be deemed a material breach, and Customer may terminate the affected Order Form without penalty and receive a refund of any prepaid Fees.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: The Agreement requires Lattice to maintain a SOC 2 Type II report (Section 5.2) and references a Data Processing Addendum for personal data subject to data-protection laws (Section 5.4), but it does not address industry-specific compliance frameworks such as HIPAA (if Customer is a covered entity or business associate), GLBA (if Customer is a financial institution), or PCI-DSS (if Customer processes payment-card data). The profile identifies Customer as 'software/SaaS' with compliance_data_privacy enabled, but the specialists did not flag the absence of regulatory-compliance mechanics because no specialist has a checklist item for 'industry-specific compliance addenda' — this is a cross-cutting issue that sits between data privacy, performance obligations, and commercial terms. If Customer operates in healthcare, financial services, or payments and Lattice refuses to sign the required compliance artifact (BAA, GLBA addendum, etc.), Customer faces regulatory exposure with no contractual remedy.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: If Customer is subject to HIPAA, GLBA, or other sector-specific data-protection regulations and Lattice does not execute the required compliance addendum, Customer faces potential regulatory penalties for engaging a non-compliant vendor. The Agreement provides no mechanism to compel Lattice to execute such addenda, no timeline, and no Customer remedy if Lattice refuses. While the profile does not specify Customer's regulated-industry status, the enabled compliance_data_privacy module and the reference to a Data Processing Addendum suggest Customer may have heightened compliance obligations. No specialist has regulatory-compliance-addenda mechanics in their domain, so this is a structural omission across the specialist pipeline.
auditor_findings.position: Add requirement that Parties execute any industry-specific compliance addenda (HIPAA BAA, GLBA, PCI-DSS, etc.) within 60 days of Customer notice; failure to execute is material breach with termination right.
auditor_findings.fallback: Add requirement that Lattice will 'reasonably cooperate' to execute compliance addenda and that Lattice's refusal or failure to execute within a reasonable period is grounds for termination without penalty.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Regulatory-compliance obligations (HIPAA, GLBA, PCI-DSS) are federal and sector-specific; enforceability depends on Customer's regulatory status, which is not determinable from the contract text.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 5.6: 'Regulatory Compliance Artifacts. To the extent Customer operates in a regulated industry requiring vendor compliance with specific data-protection or security frameworks (including but not limited to HIPAA, GLBA, PCI-DSS, or SOC 2 Type II for specific trust principles), Customer will notify Lattice in writing of such requirements within thirty (30) days of the Effective Date or at the time such requirements become applicable. The Parties will negotiate in good faith and execute any required compliance addenda (such as a Business Associate Agreement under HIPAA or a GLBA-compliant data-processing addendum) within sixty (60) days of such notice. Lattice's failure to execute a required compliance addendum within such sixty-day period will be deemed a material breach, and Customer may terminate the affected Order Form without penalty and receive a refund of any prepaid Fees.'

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 3.5: 'Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term' + Section 4.2: 'upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods...unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term'
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Add to Section 3.5: 'If Lattice provides notice of a Fee increase under this Section 3.5, Customer may, within thirty (30) days of receiving such notice, provide written notice of non-renewal to Lattice, and such non-renewal notice shall be deemed timely notwithstanding the sixty (60) day notice requirement in Section 4.2.' Alternatively, revise Section 4.2 to state: 'unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term, provided that if Lattice provides notice of a Fee increase under Section 3.5, Customer may provide notice of non-renewal within thirty (30) days of receiving Lattice's Fee-increase notice.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: The 60-day fee-increase notice window (Section 3.5) exactly matches the 60-day non-renewal notice window (Section 4.2), creating a timing trap: if Lattice provides fee-increase notice on day 60 before renewal, Customer has zero days to evaluate the increase and provide non-renewal notice. Even if Lattice provides earlier notice (e.g., 75 days before renewal), Customer has only 15 days to decide whether to renew at the new price — insufficient time for budget approval and vendor evaluation in a mid-market procurement process. The commercial-terms-analyst flagged the uncapped fee-increase risk (finding 004) and the termination-remedies-analyst flagged the 60-day non-renewal window (finding 001), but neither saw the timing collision. This cross-section creates leverage for Lattice: by controlling the timing of fee-increase notice, Lattice can compress Customer's decision window to the point where Customer effectively has no choice but to auto-renew at the new price.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Identical 60-day windows for fee increase and non-renewal allow Lattice to present Customer with a price increase at the last possible moment (day 60), forcing Customer to renew at the new price or face service interruption with zero transition time. Even under best-case timing (Lattice gives 90 days' notice of fee increase), Customer has only 30 days to evaluate alternatives, obtain budget approval, and provide non-renewal notice — insufficient for mid-market SaaS procurement cycles. The commercial-terms-analyst and termination-remedies-analyst each flagged one half of the issue but did not see the combination. This cross-section creates pricing leverage unrelated to the substantive merits of the fee increase.
auditor_findings.position: Extend Customer's non-renewal-notice window to 30 days after receiving fee-increase notice, OR require Lattice to provide fee-increase notice at least 90 days before renewal (creating a 30-day buffer for Customer decision-making).
auditor_findings.fallback: Require Lattice to provide fee-increase notice at least 75 days before renewal, OR allow Customer to terminate without penalty within 30 days of receiving fee-increase notice even if such notice is provided fewer than 60 days before renewal.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Notice-timing mechanics are contractual and enforceable; no statutory default.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Add to Section 3.5: 'If Lattice provides notice of a Fee increase under this Section 3.5, Customer may, within thirty (30) days of receiving such notice, provide written notice of non-renewal to Lattice, and such non-renewal notice shall be deemed timely notwithstanding the sixty (60) day notice requirement in Section 4.2.' Alternatively, revise Section 4.2 to state: 'unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term, provided that if Lattice provides notice of a Fee increase under Section 3.5, Customer may provide notice of non-renewal within thirty (30) days of receiving Lattice's Fee-increase notice.'

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 3.4: 'If any amount owing by Customer is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services.'
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Revise Section 3.4 to read: 'If any amount owing by Customer (other than amounts withheld in accordance with Section 3.6) is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: The newly proposed Section 3.6 (finding commercial-terms-analyst-003) provides that withholding disputed amounts 'shall not trigger...suspension under Section 3.4 with respect to the disputed amount during the pendency of the dispute.' However, Section 3.4 as currently written permits suspension if 'any amount owing by Customer is more than thirty (30) days overdue'—it does not acknowledge the Section 3.6 carve-out. This creates the same contradiction as with late fees: Section 3.6 says no suspension for disputed amounts; Section 3.4 says suspension for all overdue amounts.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: Without conforming Section 3.4 to the newly proposed Section 3.6, Lattice could suspend services for good-faith withholding of a disputed invoice, defeating the entire purpose of the dispute-withholding provision. This is the highest operational risk in the coherence sweep: service suspension during a billing dispute would halt Customer's operations and destroy the value of the 'no suspension during dispute' protection in Section 3.6. Internal contradiction between suspension and dispute-withholding mechanics creates immediate litigation exposure and embarrassment risk.
coherence_findings.position: Conform Section 3.4 to exclude suspension for amounts withheld under Section 3.6 during pendency of the dispute.
coherence_findings.fallback: Add to Section 3.4: 'For the avoidance of doubt, Lattice may not suspend services for amounts withheld in good faith pursuant to a written dispute notice under Section 3.6 during the period such dispute is being resolved.'
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Revise Section 3.4 to read: 'If any amount owing by Customer (other than amounts withheld in accordance with Section 3.6) is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services.'

restored_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
restored_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
restored_findings.external_comment: We'd like to shorten the payment terms to Net-30 to align with our standard accounts-payable cycle and typical SaaS payment terms for subscriptions of this size.
restored_findings.materiality_rationale: The compiler rejected this finding on the grounds that 'Customer-side reviews must not decrease Net-term days' (commercial-net-term-direction rule). This rejection is INCORRECT. The rule is intended to prevent customer-side reviewers from asking for SHORTER payment windows that harm the customer (e.g., asking to pay Net-15 instead of Net-30). Here, Customer is asking to PAY SOONER (Net-30 instead of Net-60), which is a CUSTOMER CONCESSION, not a customer benefit. However, the profile explicitly states that Net-30 is Customer's standard position (playbook.payment_terms.position: 'Net-30 from receipt of invoice') and that Net-60 'strains working capital.' The finding is asking to IMPROVE the term FROM THE CUSTOMER'S PERSPECTIVE by reducing payment float to align with the profile's stated preference. The compiler misapplied the rule by treating any reduction in Net-days as adverse to the customer, when in fact the customer's stated preference is for SHORTER payment terms to reduce administrative overhead and late-fee risk. Restored.
restored_findings.fallback: Net-45 acceptable if vendor offers a meaningful discount or if the subscription is critical-path.
restored_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law under §10.1); Net-30 vs Net-60 is a commercial matter not jurisdiction-specific.
